steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: “When heated to a particular temperature—around 225 degrees [blank] flaxseed oil will begin to emit smoke.” An opening dash appears after “temperature,” which means “around 225 degrees Fahrenheit” is a parenthetical interruption. The rule for dashes: a parenthetical opened with a dash must be closed with a dash, not any other punctuation mark.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Fahrenheit’: No closing punctuation. The parenthetical is left open, so “flaxseed oil will begin to emit smoke” reads as a continuation of the parenthetical rather than as the main clause. The sentence is structurally broken. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Fahrenheit—’: The closing dash matches the opening dash and seals the parenthetical. “flaxseed oil will begin to emit smoke” then correctly resumes as the main clause of the sentence. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘Fahrenheit:’: A colon cannot close a dash. The closing mark must mirror the opening punctuation; mixing a dash open with a colon close is not standard English. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Fahrenheit)’: A closing parenthesis cannot close a dash. The sentence opened the parenthetical with a dash, so only a dash can close it. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
